steps.description: State the LHS and the plan
steps.explanation: We start from the left-hand side. The denominator is $\cos\theta + \sin\theta$, so the plan is to factorise $\cos 2\theta$ to produce that same factor and then cancel it.
We use the $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$ version because it is a difference of two squares, which factorises neatly.

steps.description: Recall the difference of two squares
steps.explanation: Any difference of two squares splits into these two brackets; here $a = \cos\theta$ and $b = \sin\theta$.
Applying the rule produces the factor $(\cos\theta + \sin\theta)$, which is precisely the denominator we want to cancel.

steps.description: Rewrite the fraction with the factored numerator
steps.explanation: Substituting the factored numerator makes the shared factor $(\cos\theta + \sin\theta)$ visible top and bottom.
Dividing top and bottom by $(\cos\theta + \sin\theta)$, which is non-zero wherever the expression is defined, leaves just $\cos\theta - \sin\theta$.
The double angle has been eliminated and the LHS now equals $\cos\theta - \sin\theta$.
The simplified LHS is identical to the right-hand side we were asked to reach.
At $\theta = 30^\circ$ both sides equal $\dfrac{\sqrt3-1}{2}$, supporting the identity.
The LHS reduces to the RHS for all valid $\theta$, so the identity is proved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Writing $\cos 2\theta = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = (\cos\theta - \sin\theta)(\cos\theta + \sin\theta)$ and cancelling the common factor $(\cos\theta + \sin\theta)$ gives $\cos\theta - \sin\theta$, so $\dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{\cos\theta + \sin\theta} \equiv \cos\theta - \sin\theta$.
